title: DecodeResult::data getter clones the entire pixel buffer on every JS read
description: The `data` getter (line 27) calls `self.data.clone()`, allocating a new Vec<u8> of full decoded pixel size on every call from JavaScript. A 12MP image at RGBA8 = 48 MB cloned per getter access. wasm-bindgen will also copy the Vec into a Uint8Array on the JS side, so a single call produces two full-size allocations. The fix is to return a `js_sys::Uint8Array` view via `unsafe { js_sys::Uint8Array::view(&self.data) }` (zero-copy if the WASM memory isn't grown) or expose a `data_ptr`/`data_len` pair and let JS wrap it.

title: to_rgba RGB24 path builds the output buffer with individual push calls per pixel, causing repeated small writes
description: The RGB24 branch (lines 84-87) calls `rgba.extend_from_slice(chunk)` + `rgba.push(255)` per 3-byte pixel. While `Vec::with_capacity` avoids reallocation, the inner loop issues two separate write operations per pixel. For a typical 12MP image this is ~12M iterations with ~24M Vec write calls. An alternative is to write 4 bytes at once via an unrolled `[chunk[0], chunk[1], chunk[2], 255]` extend, or use a SIMD-friendly interleave. This is a moderate gain (5-15%) but meaningful given this is the hot path for every JPEG decode.

title: to_rgba L8 grayscale path calls extend_from_slice with a temporary 4-byte array allocation per pixel
description: The L8 branch (line 93) calls `rgba.extend_from_slice(&[lum, lum, lum, 255])` which constructs a stack-allocated `[u8; 4]` per iteration. Although the slice is stack-local, the loop still performs `pixels.len()` iterations of 4-byte writes via the slice indirection rather than writing a single u32 directly. Using `rgba.extend_from_slice` on a `u32::to_ne_bytes` or writing four bytes via `unsafe` pointer arithmetic would eliminate the per-pixel overhead.

title: decoder.info() called twice — once after read_info and once after decode — when the second call is only needed for pixel_format
description: Lines 45-47 call `decoder.info()` to obtain width/height before decoding; lines 63-65 call it again post-decode solely to get `pixel_format`. This is harmless in terms of correctness but creates an extra struct-copy per decode call. The pixel_format could be captured alongside the first `info()` call since the JPEG color space does not change between `read_info` and `decode`. Consolidating to one `info()` call eliminates a redundant struct copy.

title: No WASM timing instrumentation — decode latency per scale factor is invisible to callers
description: The `decode_scaled` function has no timing hooks. Since this is a WASM-exported entry point called from JavaScript, callers have no way to distinguish DCT-decode time from pixel-conversion time without browser profiler sampling. Adding optional `performance.now()` timestamps returned in `DecodeResult` (or a separate `DecodeStats` struct) would allow the JS layer to surface per-call latency to the scheduler's budget accounting, consistent with the rest of the pipeline's EMA-based drain tracking.
code_excerpt:
#[wasm_bindgen]
pub fn decode_scaled(jpeg: &[u8], denom: u8) -> Result<DecodeResult, JsValue> {
